topics.title: Data Types & Variables
topics.description: Java's type system: 8 primitive types (boolean, byte, short, int, long, float, double, char), reference types, type casting, widening/narrowing, autoboxing/unboxing, var (local type inference), and the String type. Understand memory layout, overflow, precision issues, and wrapper class caching.
topics.story: Imagine a storage warehouse with different-sized boxes: a shoebox for booleans (just yes/no), a small box for bytes (−128 to 127), medium boxes for int and long, and special transparent boxes for floats (you can see an approximate amount but never exact). Reference types are like a sticky note with an address — the note itself is small, but it points to a huge warehouse rack. Changing the sticky note doesn't move the rack. Autoboxing is like a robot that automatically wraps your loose coin (primitive) in a tiny coin purse (wrapper object) when you need to put it in a collection.
topics.analogy: Primitive vs Reference = Cash vs Cheque. Cash (primitive int) — you carry the actual value in your wallet. Paying transfers real value. Cheque (reference) — a piece of paper pointing to a bank account. Two people can hold cheques pointing to the same account; changing the account affects both. Autoboxing = a bank that automatically converts cash to a cheque when you put it in a safe deposit box (collection).
topics.memoryAnchor: BLSILFDC — Boolean, byte, short, int, Long, Float, Double, Char. Sizes: 1,1,2,4,8,4,8,2 bytes. Or remember: 'byte Short Int Long Float Double Char Boolean' and sizes double with each step for integers: 1→2→4→8.
topics.firstPrinciples: Why primitives at all in an OOP language? Performance and memory. An int occupies exactly 4 bytes on the stack with zero overhead. An Integer object occupies ~16 bytes on the heap (object header ~12 bytes + 4 bytes int field) plus the reference (4/8 bytes). For a 1-million-element array: int[] = 4MB, Integer[] = ~20MB + GC pressure. Java chose pragmatism over purity.
topics.bruteForce: All-reference language (Smalltalk approach): every value is an object. Simple: uniform treatment. But: 1M integers → 1M objects → 1M GC-tracked allocations → poor cache locality → slow numerical code. Java's compromise: primitives for computation, boxes for collections/generics.
topics.optimizedApproach: Use primitives (int, long, double) for local variables and arrays in hot code paths. Use wrapper types (Integer, Long, Double) only when required by APIs (generics, collections, Optional). Avoid autoboxing in tight loops — each autobox is an allocation. JVM Project Valhalla (future) aims to bring 'value types' that are primitive-like but support generics.
topics.whenToUse: Primitive when: local variables, array elements, method parameters in performance-sensitive code. Wrapper when: collections (List<Integer>), generics, null-needed fields (database nullable int → Integer), Optional<T>, stream operations (IntStream avoids boxing). Use var when: type is obvious from RHS, long generic types, local-only variables. Avoid var when: type provides important documentation context.

examples.title: Primitive Types — Size, Range, and Overflow
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":8,"lineCode":"byte b = 127;","variables":{"b":127},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"byte range: -128 to 127. 127 = 0111_1111 in binary"},{"line":9,"lineCode":"byte overflow = (byte)(b + 1);","variables":{"b":127,"overflow":-128},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"b+1 = 128 as int = 1000_0000. Cast to byte: sign bit set = -128. INTEGER OVERFLOW WRAPS around"},{"line":13,"lineCode":"int maxInt = Integer.MAX_VALUE;","variables":{"maxInt":2147483647},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"MAX_VALUE = 2^31 - 1 = 2,147,483,647 = 0x7FFFFFFF"},{"line":14,"lineCode":"System.out.println(maxInt + 1);","variables":{"maxInt":2147483647,"result":-2147483648},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"0x7FFFFFFF + 1 = 0x80000000 = -2,147,483,648 (MIN_VALUE). Overflow is SILENT in Java!"},{"line":19,"lineCode":"double d = 0.1 + 0.2;","variables":{"d":0.30000000000000004},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"IEEE 754 double: 0.1 and 0.2 cannot be represented exactly in binary. Sum has rounding error. Use BigDecimal for exact decimal arithmetic"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    subgraph Primitive Types
      B[boolean - 1 bit]
      BY[byte - 8 bits, -128..127]
      SH[short - 16 bits, -32768..32767]
      I[int - 32 bits, ±2.1B]
      L[long - 64 bits, ±9.2 quintillion]
      F[float - 32 bits, ~7 decimal digits]
      D[double - 64 bits, ~15 decimal digits]
      C[char - 16 bits, 0..65535 Unicode]
    end
    
    subgraph Overflow Behavior
      MAX[MAX_VALUE + 1] --> MIN[wraps to MIN_VALUE]
    end
examples.pseudocode:
Type sizes (bytes):
  boolean: 1    byte: 1
  short:   2    char: 2
  int:     4    float:  4
  long:    8    double: 8

Overflow: silent wrap-around (two's complement)
Float precision: IEEE 754 — use BigDecimal for money
Default values (fields): 0, 0.0, false, '\0', null
examples.explanation: Integer overflow is a silent bug — Java does NOT throw ArithmeticException for overflow (unlike division by zero). This has caused real bugs: Java's binary search had `mid = (low + high) / 2` which overflows for large arrays. Fix: `mid = low + (high - low) / 2`. Floating point imprecision is fundamental to IEEE 754 — never use == to compare doubles; use Math.abs(a - b) < epsilon.
examples.realWorldUse: Financial systems MUST use BigDecimal, not double. 0.1 + 0.2 == 0.3 is false in Java (and all IEEE 754 systems). A banking app using double for money calculations will accumulate rounding errors — pennies vanishing over millions of transactions. Java's BigDecimal provides exact decimal arithmetic at the cost of performance.

examples.title: Widening, Narrowing, and Explicit Casting
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":4,"lineCode":"byte b = 42;","variables":{"b":42},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"byte: 8 bits, value 42 = 0010_1010"},{"line":5,"lineCode":"short s = b;","variables":{"b":42,"s":42},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Widening: byte→short. Java sign-extends: 0010_1010 → 0000_0000_0010_1010. Safe, no data loss"},{"line":13,"lineCode":"int piInt = (int) pi;","variables":{"pi":3.14159,"piInt":3},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Narrowing: double→int TRUNCATES toward zero (not rounds). 3.14 → 3, 3.99 → 3, -3.7 → -3"},{"line":17,"lineCode":"int small = (int) bigLong;","variables":{"bigLong":1000000000000,"small":-727379968},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"1_000_000_000_000L in hex = 0x000000E8D4A51000. Cast to int takes lower 32 bits: 0xD4A51000 = -727379968"},{"line":26,"lineCode":"float approx = precise;","variables":{"precise":123456789012345,"approx":1.2345679E14},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Widening long→float requires NO cast, but float has only 23-bit mantissa — loses precision for large values"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    subgraph Widening Conversions
      B[byte] --> S[short] --> I[int] --> L[long] --> F[float] --> D[double]
      C[char] --> I
    end
    
    subgraph Narrowing Requires Cast
      D2[double] -->|cast| F2[float]
      F2 -->|cast| L2[long]
      L2 -->|cast| I2[int]
      I2 -->|cast| S2[short]
      S2 -->|cast| B2[byte]
    end
    
    style Narrowing Requires Cast fill:#ffeeee
examples.pseudocode:
Widening (automatic):
  byte → short → int → long → float → double
  char → int
  (JVM zero/sign extends as needed)

Narrowing (explicit cast):
  (int) 3.7 = 3    (truncate, NOT round)
  (byte) 200 = -56 (upper bits dropped)
  (int) 1_000_000_000_000L = garbage (upper 32 bits dropped)
examples.explanation: The dangerous widening: long→float and long→double are widening (no cast needed) but CAN lose precision. float has only 23 bits of mantissa (~7 decimal digits) so large longs can't be represented exactly. This is a silent precision loss without compile-time warning — one of Java's gotchas. Always test edge cases when converting between numeric types.
examples.realWorldUse: Classic bug in financial code: `float totalAmount = (float) longAmount / 100;` — two precision losses: long→float drops precision, then division further compounds. Fix: use BigDecimal(longAmount).divide(BigDecimal.valueOf(100)). In Android, sensor data is often float — converting to int for display should use Math.round() not (int) cast.

examples.title: Autoboxing, Unboxing, and Integer Cache
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":3,"lineCode":"Integer boxed = 42;","variables":{"boxed":"Integer@cache[42]"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Autoboxing: compiler inserts Integer.valueOf(42). Returns cached object for -128..127"},{"line":7,"lineCode":"Integer a = 127;","variables":{"a":"Integer@cache[127]"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"127 is in cache range (-128..127). Integer.valueOf(127) returns same cached object every time"},{"line":9,"lineCode":"Integer c = 128;","variables":{"c":"Integer@0xABC"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"128 is ABOVE cache range. Integer.valueOf(128) = new Integer(128). New object each time"},{"line":12,"lineCode":"a == b","variables":{"result":true},"phase":"CONDITION_TRUE","annotation":"a and b point to same cached object. Reference equality (==) is true. This is the TRAP — seems to work for small numbers"},{"line":13,"lineCode":"c == d","variables":{"result":false},"phase":"CONDITION_FALSE","annotation":"c and d are different objects (both 128 but not cached). == compares references → false. ALWAYS use .equals() for Integer comparison!"},{"line":19,"lineCode":"int x = nullInt;","variables":{"nullInt":null},"phase":"THROW","annotation":"Unboxing null: compiler inserts nullInt.intValue(). Calling .intValue() on null → NullPointerException"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[int 42] -->|autobox: Integer.valueOf(42)| B[Integer object]
    B -->|unbox: .intValue()| C[int 42]
    
    subgraph Integer Cache
      D[-128 to 127]
      D -->|cached: same object| E[valueOf returns cached ref]
      F[128 and above]
      F -->|new object| G[valueOf creates new Integer]
    end
    
    H[null Integer] -->|unbox| I[NullPointerException!]
examples.pseudocode:
Autoboxing = Integer.valueOf(primitive):
  if -128 <= value <= 127: return cached object
  else: return new Integer(value)

Unboxing = wrapper.intValue():
  if wrapper == null: throw NullPointerException

Same for: Long (-128..127), Short (-128..127),
  Byte (all), Character (0..127), Boolean (TRUE/FALSE)
examples.explanation: The Integer cache (-128 to 127) exists because these values are extremely common. JLS guarantees this range is cached. The upper bound can be extended with -XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=N JVM flag. This is a famous Java interview gotcha: `Integer i1 = 1000; Integer i2 = 1000; i1 == i2` is false. The rule: NEVER use == to compare wrapper objects — always use .equals() or compare the unboxed primitives.
examples.realWorldUse: In Spring Data JPA, entity fields using Integer/Long can be null (maps to SQL NULL). Using int would prevent null representation. The trade-off: int is faster, Integer supports null. For loop indices and computation, always use primitives. For JPA fields that can be null (optional FK columns), use wrapper types. For performance-critical list operations, use IntStream instead of Stream<Integer>.

examples.title: String Immutability and String Pool
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":3,"lineCode":"String s = \"hello\";","variables":{"s":"ref→Pool[hello]"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"String literal \"hello\" placed in String Pool on heap. s holds a reference to the pool entry"},{"line":4,"lineCode":"String upper = s.toUpperCase();","variables":{"s":"ref→Pool[hello]","upper":"ref→Heap[HELLO]"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"toUpperCase() creates a NEW String object. Original 'hello' is unchanged — String is immutable"},{"line":9,"lineCode":"String a = \"java\";","variables":{"a":"ref→Pool[java]"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Literal \"java\" interned in String Pool"},{"line":10,"lineCode":"String b = \"java\";","variables":{"b":"ref→Pool[java]"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Same literal — JVM reuses the same Pool entry. a and b point to SAME object"},{"line":11,"lineCode":"String c = new String(\"java\");","variables":{"c":"ref→Heap@0x300"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"new String() bypasses Pool. Creates separate heap object even though content is identical to a"},{"line":20,"lineCode":"result += i;","variables":{"result":"new String each iteration"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"String concatenation in loop: each += creates new String. 5 iterations = 5 garbage objects. O(n²) time complexity"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    subgraph String Pool
      P1[\"hello\"]
      P2[\"java\"]
    end
    subgraph Heap
      H1[new String(\"java\") @ 0x300]
    end
    A[var s] --> P1
    B[var a] --> P2
    C[var b] --> P2
    D[var c] --> H1
    
    E[a == b] -->|true, same ref| OK
    F[a == c] -->|false, diff ref| WRONG
examples.pseudocode:
String immutability:
  every method returns a NEW String
  original never modified

String Pool:
  literals automatically interned
  new String() bypasses pool
  .intern() adds to pool

Performance:
  + in loop: O(n²) time (copies each time)
  StringBuilder: O(n) time (internal char buffer, resizes like ArrayList)
examples.explanation: String immutability enables thread safety (immutable objects are inherently thread-safe), security (can't be modified after validation), and caching (safe to use as HashMap keys). The String Pool saves memory for repeated strings. StringBuilder (single-thread) and StringBuffer (thread-safe) are the mutable alternatives. In Java 9+, String internally uses byte[] (compact strings) instead of char[] for Latin-1 strings, halving memory for ASCII content.
examples.realWorldUse: In HTTP request processing, parsing headers involves many string operations. Using + in a loop for building responses is a classic performance issue. StringBuilder is 10-50x faster for repeated concatenation. In Java 8+, the compiler optimizes single-expression string concats with StringBuilder automatically, but NOT across loop iterations — that still requires explicit StringBuilder.

examples.title: var — Local Type Inference
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":6,"lineCode":"var list = new ArrayList<String>();","variables":{"list":"ArrayList<String>"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"var infers ArrayList<String> from RHS. Type is fixed at compile time — this is NOT dynamic typing"},{"line":10,"lineCode":"var msg = \"hello\";","variables":{"msg":"String"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"var infers String — not Object or CharSequence. The actual declared type in bytecode IS String"},{"line":14,"lineCode":"for (var n : numbers)","variables":{"n":"Integer"},"phase":"LOOP_START","annotation":"Loop variable n inferred as Integer (element type of List<Integer>). Useful for complex generic types"},{"line":21,"lineCode":"var y = null;","variables":{},"phase":"DECLARE","annotation":"COMPILE ERROR: var requires type inference from RHS. null has no type — compiler cannot infer. Must write: String y = null"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[var x = expr] -->|compiler infers| B[Exact type of expr at compile time]
    B --> C[Type fixed — cannot change]
    
    D[var y = null] -->|cannot infer from null| E[COMPILE ERROR]
    
    subgraph Where var works
      F[Local variable declarations]
      G[for-each loop variables]
      H[try-with-resources]
    end
    
    subgraph Where var does NOT work
      I[Method parameters]
      J[Return types]
      K[Class fields]
    end
examples.pseudocode:
var = compile-time type inference for local variables

Rules:
  1. Must have initializer (var x; is illegal)
  2. Initializer must not be null
  3. Only for local variables, for-loop vars, try-with-resources
  4. Type is the STATIC type of the initializer expression
  5. Type is fixed after inference — var is NOT dynamic

Bytecode: var disappears — replaced with actual type
examples.explanation: var was introduced in Java 10 (JEP 286). It reduces boilerplate for obvious types (new ArrayList<String>()) without sacrificing type safety. The inferred type is the declared type — not Object. var is local-only deliberately: method signatures are API contracts that benefit from explicit types for documentation. Avoid var when the type isn't obvious from the RHS — `var result = process()` tells the reader nothing about what process() returns.
examples.realWorldUse: var shines with complex generics: `Map<String, List<Map<Integer, Set<String>>>> map = new HashMap<>()` becomes `var map = new HashMap<String, List<Map<Integer, Set<String>>>>()`. Still verbose on the right, but better than duplicating on the left. In streams and lambdas: `var employees = getEmployees().stream().filter(...).collect(...)` — the stream type is obvious from context.

problems.title: Integer Overflow Detection
problems.description:
Detect if adding two integers will overflow `Integer.MAX_VALUE` or underflow `Integer.MIN_VALUE` before performing the addition.

**Example:**
willOverflow(Integer.MAX_VALUE, 1) => true
willOverflow(1000, 2000) => false
willUnderflow(Integer.MIN_VALUE, -1) => true

**Key insight:** Check `a > 0 && b > Integer.MAX_VALUE - a` to detect overflow without actually overflowing.
problems.hint1: For addition: overflow occurs when a > 0 && b > 0 && result < 0, OR a < 0 && b < 0 && result > 0.
problems.hint2: Alternatively, use Math.addExact(a, b) which already throws ArithmeticException on overflow.
problems.hint3: For multiplication: cast to long, multiply, check if result fits in int range (Integer.MIN_VALUE to MAX_VALUE).
problems.constraints: Must throw ArithmeticException on overflow. Use Math.addExact/multiplyExact or manual long-cast check.
problems.editorial: Integer overflow wraps around silently — no exception. Use Math.addExact(a, b) to throw ArithmeticException on overflow. Check: (a > 0 && b > Integer.MAX_VALUE - a) before adding. Or detect post-addition: if (a > 0 && b > 0 && result < 0) then overflow occurred.

problems.title: Type Range Validator
problems.description:
Validate whether a value fits within a given primitive type range.

**Example:**
fitsInByte(127) => true
fitsInByte(128) => false
fitsInShort(32767) => true
fitsInShort(32768) => false
fitsInInt(Long.MAX_VALUE) => false

**Key insight:** Byte: -128 to 127. Short: -32768 to 32767. Int: -2^31 to 2^31-1. Long: -2^63 to 2^63-1.
problems.hint1: Check against Byte.MIN_VALUE/MAX_VALUE, then Short, then Integer, then Long.
problems.hint2: Use if-else chain from smallest to largest type. Return the first type that can hold the value.
problems.hint3: For 'BigInteger' case: since the parameter is already a long, values always fit in long. Use BigDecimal.valueOf for the check.
problems.constraints: Use MIN_VALUE/MAX_VALUE constants from wrapper classes. Return exact smallest fitting type.
problems.editorial: Each primitive has MIN_VALUE and MAX_VALUE constants. byte: -128 to 127. short: -32768 to 32767. int: ~-2.1B to 2.1B. long: ~-9.2E18 to 9.2E18. Use Long.parseLong and catch NumberFormatException. Compare against the type's actual bounds, not Double (floating point imprecision).

problems.title: Float Precision Comparator
problems.description:
Compare floating-point numbers correctly using an epsilon tolerance instead of `==`.

**Example:**
0.1 + 0.2 == 0.3 => false (floating point imprecision)
equalsApprox(0.1 + 0.2, 0.3, 1e-9) => true

**Key insight:** Never compare floats/doubles with `==`. Use `Math.abs(a - b) < epsilon`. For financial calculations, use `BigDecimal`.
problems.hint1: Use Math.abs(a - b) <= epsilon for the comparison.
problems.hint2: For relative epsilon (better for large values): Math.abs(a - b) <= epsilon * Math.max(Math.abs(a), Math.abs(b)).
problems.hint3: epsilon is typically 1e-9 for double, 1e-6 for float.
problems.constraints: Must use Math.abs(). epsilon must be a parameter (not hardcoded). Show both false (direct ==) and true (epsilon comparison).
problems.editorial: Never use == or - for floating-point equality. Use Math.abs(a - b) < epsilon where epsilon is the acceptable tolerance (e.g., 1e-9). BigDecimal.compareTo() returns 0 for equal values — use it for exact decimal comparison. Double.compare() handles NaN and -0.0 correctly.

problems.title: Autoboxing Cache Trap
problems.description:
Predict the output of Integer autoboxing cache comparisons:

```java
Integer a = 127; Integer b = 127;
a == b => true  // cached (-128 to 127)
Integer c = 128; Integer d = 128;
c == d => false // new objects outside cache
```

Implement `predictBoxingEquality(int value)` returning whether `Integer.valueOf(value) == Integer.valueOf(value)`.
problems.hint1: Integer cache range is -128 to 127 (guaranteed by JLS). Values in this range always return the same object.
problems.hint2: Use == for reference comparison and .equals() for value comparison.
problems.hint3: The cache boundary is exactly at 127 — Integer 127 == Integer 127 (true), Integer 128 == Integer 128 (false).
problems.constraints: Must use Integer (wrapper) not int. Must demonstrate cache boundary at 127/128 exactly.
problems.editorial: Integer.valueOf() caches values from -128 to 127. Comparing cached Integer objects with == returns true because they're the same instance. Outside this range, new Integer objects are created — == returns false. Always use .equals() for Integer comparison, never ==.

problems.title: String to Primitive Conversions
problems.description:
Convert strings to each primitive type with proper error handling.

**Example:**
parseInt("42") => 42
parseDouble("3.14") => 3.14
parseBoolean("true") => true
parseInt("abc") => throws NumberFormatException
parseInt(null) => throws NumberFormatException

Implement `safeParse(String s, Class<?> type)` returning `Optional<?>` wrapping the result.
problems.hint1: Use Integer.parseInt() inside try-catch(NumberFormatException) to handle invalid input.
problems.hint2: For parseBoolean: use s.equalsIgnoreCase() to check for all accepted values.
problems.hint3: Return the defaultVal if parsing throws NumberFormatException.
problems.constraints: Handle null input. Use try-catch for NumberFormatException. parseBoolean must support true/false/yes/no/1/0 case-insensitively.
problems.editorial: Integer.parseInt() throws NumberFormatException for invalid strings. Double.parseDouble() is similar. Boolean.parseBoolean() is lenient — only 'true' (case-insensitive) returns true, everything else (including null) returns false. Character.getNumericValue() converts char digits to int.

problems.title: Bitwise Type Analysis
problems.description:
Analyze a number's binary representation using bitwise operators.

**Example:**
int n = 42; // binary: 00101010
binaryString(n) => "00101010"
countBits(n) => 8
countSetBits(n) => 3 (bits 1,3,5 are set)
highestSetBit(n) => 5 (0-indexed from right)

**Key insight:** `Integer.toBinaryString(n)` shows binary. `Integer.bitCount(n)` counts set bits.
problems.hint1: Use Integer.toBinaryString() for binary, Integer.toHexString() for hex, Integer.toOctalString() for octal.
problems.hint2: Pad binary to 32 bits with leading zeros: String.format("%32s", binary).replace(' ', '0').
problems.hint3: Sign bit is bit 31 (leftmost). If binary representation starts with '1', the number is negative.
problems.constraints: Binary must be exactly 32 characters (padded with zeros). Hex must use uppercase with 0x prefix. Sign bit must be identified.
problems.editorial: Boolean: 1 bit logical, 1 byte storage. byte: 8-bit signed (-128 to 127). char: 16-bit unsigned UTF-16. Use Integer.bitCount() for set bits, Integer.toBinaryString() for binary representation. Type hierarchy: byte → short → int → long → float → double (widening conversions are implicit).

problems.title: StringBuilder Performance Benchmark
problems.description:
Benchmark `String` concatenation with `+` vs `StringBuilder` for 10,000 iterations.

**Example:**
benchmark(10000):
concatWithPlus: ~200ms (creates 10000 String objects)
concatWithBuilder: ~2ms (one StringBuilder)

Return: `Map<String, Long>` with {"plus_ms": X, "builder_ms": Y}

**Key insight:** `s += part` inside a loop is O(n^2) — each iteration copies the growing string. StringBuilder is O(n).
problems.hint1: Use System.nanoTime() to measure. Run each approach multiple times for warmth.
problems.hint2: String + in a loop creates a new String each iteration: O(n²) time and space.
problems.hint3: StringBuilder maintains a char[] buffer and resizes geometrically: O(n) amortized.
problems.constraints: Must measure actual elapsed time. Ratio must be > 10 for N=10,000. Include JIT warm-up calls.
problems.editorial: StringBuilder is O(n) amortized for append — it doubles capacity when full (similar to ArrayList). String concatenation in a loop creates O(n) intermediate String objects. StringBuilder.toString() creates one final String. For thread-safe use, StringBuffer (synchronized version) or join at the end.

problems.title: Type Conversion Matrix
problems.description:
Build a type conversion matrix showing implicit widening and explicit narrowing between all primitive types.

**Example:**
byte -> short -> int -> long -> float -> double (widening, implicit)
double -> float -> long -> int -> short -> byte (narrowing, requires cast)

canImplicitlyConvert(int.class, double.class) => true
canImplicitlyConvert(double.class, int.class) => false
needsExplicitCast(double.class, int.class) => true
problems.hint1: Cast the double to each type: (int) value, (long) value, (float) value, (byte) value, etc.
problems.hint2: Special cases: (int) Double.NaN = 0, (int) Double.POSITIVE_INFINITY = Integer.MAX_VALUE.
problems.hint3: Use a LinkedHashMap to preserve insertion order. Key = type name, Value = converted value as String.
problems.constraints: Handle NaN and Infinity cases. char must only be shown if value is in valid Unicode range (0-65535).
problems.editorial: Widening conversions are implicit and safe (except long→float which can lose precision). Narrowing conversions require explicit cast and can lose data. long→float: a 64-bit long can have more significant digits than a 32-bit float's mantissa (23 bits) — precision loss occurs for large values.

problems.title: BigDecimal Money Calculator
problems.description:
Calculate monetary amounts using `BigDecimal` to avoid floating-point precision errors.

**Example:**
// WRONG: double
0.1 + 0.2 => 0.30000000000000004

// CORRECT: BigDecimal
new BigDecimal("0.10").add(new BigDecimal("0.20")) => 0.30

Implement `calculateTotal(String[] prices)` summing price strings as BigDecimal, rounding to 2 decimal places.
problems.hint1: Create BigDecimal from String (not double!): new BigDecimal('0.1') is exact, new BigDecimal(0.1) is not.
problems.hint2: Use BigDecimal.add(), divide(divisor, scale, RoundingMode.HALF_UP).
problems.hint3: For splitBill: share = total.divide(N, 2, HALF_UP). remainder = total - (share * N).
problems.constraints: Must use BigDecimal(String) constructor, NOT BigDecimal(double). Use RoundingMode.HALF_UP for financial rounding.
problems.editorial: BigDecimal stores exact decimal values — no floating-point rounding errors. Always use BigDecimal(String) constructor, not BigDecimal(double) (which inherits the double's imprecision). Use add(), subtract(), multiply(), divide() — not + - * /. setScale() controls decimal places. Use RoundingMode.HALF_UP for money.

problems.title: Char Array Password Handling
problems.hint1: Strings are immutable — you cannot zero them out. They may stay in String Pool indefinitely.
problems.hint2: char[] is mutable — you can overwrite all elements with '\0' after use.
problems.hint3: Java's JPasswordField.getPassword() returns char[] for this exact reason.
problems.constraints: char[] version must use Arrays.fill(password, '\0') in finally block. Must explain why String is less secure.
problems.editorial: char[] is preferred over String for passwords because char[] can be zeroed (Arrays.fill(pwd, '\0')) after use. String is immutable — the sensitive value persists in the String Pool until GC. This is why java.security.spec.KeySpec uses char[] for passwords (PBKDF2WithHmacSHA256).

problems.title: var Usage Analyzer
problems.description:
Analyze code to find where `var` (local type inference) can be used appropriately vs where it harms readability.

**Example:**
var list = new ArrayList<String>(); // OK — type obvious from RHS
var x = process(); // BAD — return type unclear
var entry = map.entrySet().iterator().next(); // OK — verbose type

Implement `canUseVar(String declaration)` returning "OK", "BAD", or "BORDERLINE" with reason.
problems.hint1: Use regex or simple string parsing. var declarations match pattern: `var <name> = <expr>;`
problems.hint2: Classify by RHS: if RHS starts with 'new ' → good use. If RHS contains '(' → method call (potentially unclear).
problems.hint3: Count occurrences of 'var ' at word boundary in each line.
problems.constraints: Must count total var declarations and classify by RHS type. Use regex for detection.
problems.editorial: var (local variable type inference, Java 10+) infers the type at compile time — it's not dynamic typing. The type is fixed and cannot change. var works only for local variables with initializer, for-each loop variables, and try-with-resources. Cannot use var for fields, parameters, or return types.

problems.title: Primitive Array vs Wrapper Array Memory
problems.description:
Measure memory usage difference between `int[]` and `Integer[]`:

**Example:**
int[] primitives = new int[1000000];    // ~4MB
Integer[] boxed = new Integer[1000000]; // ~16MB+ (header per object)

Implement `measureArrayMemory(int size)` returning `{primitive_bytes, boxed_bytes}`.

**Key insight:** Each `Integer` object has ~16 bytes header + 4 bytes value. Primitive `int` is 4 bytes. 4x+ overhead from boxing.
problems.hint1: int[N]: 16 bytes overhead (object header + length) + N * 4 bytes = ~4MB for 1M elements.
problems.hint2: Integer[N]: 16 bytes overhead + N * 4 bytes (references) + N * 16 bytes (Integer objects) = ~20MB.
problems.hint3: Measure with System.gc() + Runtime before/after allocation to see real heap delta.
problems.constraints: Must measure actual heap delta. int[] should use ~4MB, Integer[] should use ~5-20MB depending on Integer cache hits.
problems.editorial: int[] stores primitive ints contiguously — no boxing, no object overhead. Integer[] stores references to Integer objects on the heap — 16 bytes per object plus 4 bytes per reference. For large arrays, int[] uses ~4x less memory and has better cache locality. Use primitive arrays for performance-critical code.

problems.title: String Intern Pool Stress Test
problems.description:
Stress-test the String intern pool by interning thousands of strings and measuring memory:

**Example:**
String.intern() adds to the pool — repeated interning of the same string reuses the pooled instance.
"hello".intern() == "hello" => true (same reference from pool)

Implement `internStressTest(int count)` interning `count` unique strings and return pool size estimate.
problems.hint1: Create 10,000 strings using new String("prefix" + i) to bypass the pool, then intern them.
problems.hint2: After interning two strings with same content, they should have == equality.
problems.hint3: Measure heap before/after to see memory savings from interning duplicate strings.

testCases.output: Without intern (key-0 at idx 0 vs 10): false
With intern    (key-0 at idx 0 vs 10): true

problems.constraints: Must use new String() to bypass pool initially. .intern() must produce == equality for same-content strings.
problems.editorial: String.intern() returns the pooled reference. After intern, == comparison works for equal strings. The JDK interns all string literals automatically. Calling intern() on dynamically created strings moves them to the pool — can cause metaspace growth if abused. Modern JVMs (Java 7+) move the pool to the heap.

problems.title: Type Widening Surprise: long to float
problems.hint1: float has 23-bit mantissa = ~7 decimal digits. long values > 10^7 can't be represented exactly as float.
problems.hint2: Find precision loss: for (long v = 1; v == (long)(float)v; v++) — loop until round-trip fails.
problems.hint3: Magnitude of error: Math.abs(original - (long)(float)original).
problems.constraints: Must find exact first precision loss value (2^24+1 = 16,777,217). Must show that widening can lose precision.
problems.editorial: long (64-bit) to float (32-bit): float has only 23 bits of mantissa, so large longs (> 2^24) lose precision when converted. Example: (float) 16777217L == 16777216.0f — the last bit is lost. This is a widening conversion that can still lose information — unique among Java's widening conversions.

problems.title: Custom Numeric Type — Safe Integer
problems.description:
Create a `SafeInt` class that wraps `int` and throws `ArithmeticException` on overflow instead of silently wrapping.

**Example:**
SafeInt.add(Integer.MAX_VALUE, 1) => throws ArithmeticException("Integer overflow")
SafeInt.add(100, 200) => 300
SafeInt.multiply(100000, 100000) => throws ArithmeticException (> MAX_VALUE)
problems.hint1: Use Math.addExact, subtractExact, multiplyExact — they throw ArithmeticException on overflow.
problems.hint2: Store value as private final int. Implement add(SafeInt other), subtract(SafeInt other), multiply(SafeInt other) returning new SafeInt.
problems.hint3: For Comparable: Integer.compare(this.value, other.value). For equals: check class and compare values.
problems.constraints: Must use Math.addExact/subtractExact/multiplyExact. Must implement equals, hashCode, compareTo correctly. value must be final.
problems.editorial: Wrap int in a class with overflow detection on all arithmetic operations. throw ArithmeticException on overflow. Use Math.addExact, Math.subtractExact, Math.multiplyExact internally. Expose a value() accessor returning the int. This is the pattern behind Guava's IntMath.checkedAdd().

problems.title: Generic Number Accumulator
problems.description:
Implement `NumberAccumulator<T extends Number>` that sums any `Number` subtype using `doubleValue()`.

**Example:**
accumulator.add(1); accumulator.add(2.5); accumulator.add(3L);
accumulator.sum() => 6.5
accumulator.average() => 2.1667
accumulator.count() => 3

**Key insight:** All `Number` subtypes have `doubleValue()` — use it for universal accumulation.
problems.hint1: Use T extends Number to accept any Number subclass. Call .doubleValue() for addition.
problems.hint2: For precise BigDecimal accumulation, check if T is BigDecimal and use a different code path.
problems.hint3: Use List<T> parameter. Use a forEach loop calling doubleValue() to accumulate sum.
problems.constraints: Must use T extends Number. Must call .doubleValue() for double sum. Must use BigDecimal arithmetic for preciseSum.
problems.editorial: T extends Number gives access to intValue(), doubleValue(), etc. Use a List<T> to store values. sum(): iterate calling doubleValue(). getMin()/getMax(): iterate with comparisons. The bounded type parameter enables type-safe generic numeric code that works for Integer, Double, BigDecimal.

problems.title: Unsigned Integer Operations
problems.description:
Implement unsigned integer operations in Java (which has no unsigned int primitive):

**Example:**
unsignedToLong(-1) => 4294967295L (max unsigned 32-bit)
unsignedAdd(Integer.MAX_VALUE, 1) => 2147483648L (as unsigned)
unsignedCompare(-1, 1) => positive (in unsigned, -1 is max value)

**Key insight:** `Integer.toUnsignedLong(n)` converts to unsigned. `Integer.compareUnsigned(a, b)` treats both as unsigned.
problems.hint1: Integer.MAX_VALUE = 2^31-1 = 2,147,483,647. As unsigned, 0xFFFFFFFF = 4,294,967,295.
problems.hint2: Use Integer.toUnsignedString(int) to print int as unsigned. Integer.toUnsignedLong(int) to get unsigned long value.
problems.hint3: Integer.compareUnsigned(a, b) treats both as unsigned. -1 unsigned = 4,294,967,295 which is > any positive int.
problems.constraints: Must use Integer.toUnsignedString(), Integer.compareUnsigned(), Integer.divideUnsigned(). Show signed vs unsigned difference for -1.
problems.editorial: Java has no unsigned integer type. Simulate with long (use long for unsigned int). Integer.toUnsignedString() and Integer.compareUnsigned() treat the int bits as unsigned. For unsigned right shift, use >>> (zero-fills high bits) instead of >> (sign-extends). Long.toUnsignedString() for unsigned long.

problems.title: Variable Shadowing Detector
problems.hint1: Parse the source to find: (1) class-level fields, (2) method parameters and local variables. A shadowing occurs when a parameter/local has the same name as a field.
problems.hint2: Use regex to find field declarations (matching `private/public/protected type name;`) and parameter names.
problems.hint3: Return list of `ShadowReport(fieldName, location)` records.

testCases.input: class with name and age fields shadowed in constructor

problems.constraints: Must detect field-to-parameter shadowing. Use regex for parsing. Return list of ShadowReport records.
problems.editorial: Shadowing: a local variable with the same name as a field or outer-class member hides the outer one. this.x always refers to the field. Detect with reflection: compare Method.getParameters() names against Field names in the class. Shadowing is legal but confusing — many style guides forbid it.
problems.sampleInput: outer int x=10; inner int x=20;
problems.sampleOutput: compile error: variable x already defined

problems.title: Runtime Type Hierarchy Walker
problems.description:
Walk the type hierarchy of a given class using reflection: superclass chain + all implemented interfaces (recursively).

**Example:**
walkHierarchy(ArrayList.class)
=> ArrayList -> AbstractList -> AbstractCollection -> Object
=> implements: List, RandomAccess, Cloneable, Serializable

Implement `getFullHierarchy(Class<?> cls)` returning a `Map<String, List<String>>` with {superclasses, interfaces}.
problems.hint1: Use clazz.getSuperclass() to walk up the class hierarchy. Use clazz.getInterfaces() to get directly implemented interfaces.
problems.hint2: Recursively collect interfaces: each interface may extend other interfaces via .getInterfaces().
problems.hint3: Use a Set<Class<?>> to avoid printing duplicates in diamond inheritance hierarchies.

testCases.output: contains ArrayList and AbstractList and List

problems.constraints: Must use getSuperclass() and getInterfaces(). Must stop at Object (getSuperclass() returns null for Object). Must avoid infinite loops for interfaces extending interfaces.
problems.editorial: Walk the type hierarchy: cls.getSuperclass() for parent classes, cls.getInterfaces() for interfaces. Recurse both. Use a Set<Class<?>> visited to avoid infinite loops (interfaces can be implemented multiple times via different paths). Collect all types in a List<String> of getSimpleName().

problems.title: Comprehensive Type System Quiz Engine
problems.description:
Build a quiz engine that presents type system questions and evaluates answers:

**Example questions:**
1. "What is the range of byte?" => "-128 to 127"
2. "Which is wider: long or float?" => "float (can represent larger magnitudes but with less precision)"
3. "What does Integer.MAX_VALUE + 1 equal?" => Integer.MIN_VALUE (overflow)

Implement `QuizEngine` with `nextQuestion()` and `checkAnswer(String answer)` methods.
problems.hint1: Use a List<Question> where Question is a record with: code, expectedOutput, explanation.
problems.hint2: The check method compares user's answer (trimmed) with expectedOutput (trimmed) case-insensitively.
problems.hint3: Include a mix of topics: overflow, autoboxing cache, string ==, casting truncation, null unboxing.
problems.constraints: Must have exactly 10 questions. Must cover: overflow, autoboxing cache, string pool, casting, floating point, var. checkAnswer must be case-insensitive.
problems.editorial: Build a quiz engine with a Map<String, Runnable> of test cases. Each test verifies a specific type behavior (overflow, autoboxing cache, float precision). Use assert or custom check() method to report pass/fail. Return a score. This consolidates all type-system edge cases into a single runnable spec.
